label: Lagrange Multiplier
definition: A method for optimizing a function subject to constraints by introducing a new variable for each constraint, converting a constrained problem into an unconstrained stationarity condition and revealing the price of each constraint.

body.text: A Lagrange multiplier is the device that turns a constrained optimization problem into an unconstrained one. Suppose you want to minimize a function while obeying an equality constraint that confines you to a surface. At the constrained optimum you cannot move in any allowed direction and improve, which forces a geometric coincidence: the gradient of the objective must point in the same direction as the gradient of the constraint, since any component of the objective's gradient lying along the surface would let you descend further. The Lagrange multiplier is the scalar that expresses this proportionality. Joseph-Louis Lagrange's insight was to fold it into a single combined function, the Lagrangian, formed as the objective minus the multiplier times the constraint, whose ordinary stationary points (where its gradient is zero) recover exactly the constrained optima together with the constraints themselves.

Mechanically, you introduce one multiplier per constraint, build the Lagrangian, and set all its partial derivatives to zero, those with respect to the original variables and those with respect to the multipliers. The derivatives with respect to the variables impose the gradient-alignment condition; the derivatives with respect to the multipliers reproduce the constraints. The result is a single system of equations encoding both the objective's stationarity and feasibility, solvable by the same calculus as any unconstrained problem. For inequality constraints the method generalizes to the Karush-Kuhn-Tucker conditions, which add the requirement that each multiplier be nonnegative and that it be zero whenever its constraint is slack, the complementary-slackness rule that says only the constraints actively pressing on the solution carry a nonzero price.

That word price is the deepest interpretation. A Lagrange multiplier measures the sensitivity of the optimal value to its constraint: it is the rate at which the best achievable objective would improve if the constraint were loosened by one unit, a shadow price in the language of economics. A binding constraint with a large multiplier is one that is costing you a great deal; a constraint with a zero multiplier is not limiting you at all. This sensitivity reading is what makes multipliers indispensable far beyond mechanically solving for an optimum, and it is the foundation of duality in convex-optimization, where the multipliers become the dual variables and the dual problem is built entirely from them.

In machine learning the method is pervasive, often beneath the surface. The dual formulation of the support-vector-machine introduces a multiplier for each training example, and the optimal multipliers turn out to be nonzero only for the support vectors, the points on the margin, which is both why the solution is sparse and why the kernel trick applies. Regularization has a multiplier reading too: training subject to a bound on a model's norm is equivalent, through a multiplier, to adding a penalty term, which is the formal link between constrained capacity and the familiar weight-decay penalty. The entropy-regularized form of optimal-transport solved by the Sinkhorn algorithm is derived by attaching multipliers to the marginal constraints, and those multipliers are exactly the Kantorovich potentials.

The same idea governs constraints throughout statistics and physics. Deriving the maximum-entropy distribution under moment constraints, which yields the Gaussian, the exponential, and the softmax forms, is a Lagrange-multiplier calculation, with each multiplier setting the weight of a constraint in the resulting exponential family. In physics the Lagrangian and its multipliers encode conservation laws and constrained dynamics. The unifying message for a practitioner is that constraints are not obstacles to be worked around but quantities with prices, and the Lagrange multiplier is the tool that both enforces a constraint and tells you precisely what it costs, which is why it sits at the center of constrained optimization and its duality theory.

image.alt: Concentric dashed level curves of an objective function f, each with small gradient arrows pointing inward, crossed by a red constraint curve g equals c; the red and dashed curves are tangent where the constrained optimum sits.
image.caption: The geometry behind Lagrange multipliers. The dashed curves are level sets of the objective f, and the red curve is the constraint g equals c. At the constrained optimum the constraint curve is tangent to a level curve, so the two gradients line up; the multiplier is the scalar that expresses how much one gradient must be scaled to equal the other. Where the curves merely cross, you can still slide along the constraint to a better value.
